title: Advanced Cervical Cancer
chief_complaint: Postcoital bleeding and foul-smelling vaginal discharge for 6 months. No Pap smear in 15 years.

physical_exam.Pelvic: Friable exophytic cervical mass 5cm, bleeds on contact. Upper vagina involved. Left parametrium fixed.

hidden_data.cervical_biopsy: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, HPV-related, p16 positive
hidden_data.mri_pelvis: 5cm cervical mass with parametrial invasion (Stage IIB). Pelvic lymphadenopathy. No hydronephrosis.
hidden_data.pet_ct: Hypermetabolic cervical mass + pelvic/para-aortic nodes. No distant metastases.

correct_diagnosis: Invasive Squamous Cell Carcinoma of Cervix - FIGO Stage IIB, HPV 16 Positive
key_learning_points: HPV causes 99.7% of cervical cancers. HPV 16 (50%) and 18 (20%) cause 70% of cases
Risk: Multiple sexual partners, Early first intercourse, Immunosuppression, Smoking, Lack of screening
Screening: Pap smear age 21-29 q3 years. Age 30-65: Pap+HPV co-testing q5 years (preferred). HPV vaccination: Gardasil 9 for all girls/boys age 9-12 (through age 26, shared decision-making 27-45)
FIGO staging: Clinical (not surgical). Stage IIB = Parametrial invasion. Treatment: Definitive chemoradiation (Cisplatin-sensitized external beam + brachytherapy) for Stage IIB-IVA
Prognosis: Stage I 80-95%, Stage II 60-80%. Post-treatment surveillance: Physical exam q3-6 months ×2 years, then q6-12 months. Annual cytology. PET-CT at 3-6 months post-treatment

feedback.attending_note: GYN oncology and radiation oncology referrals. Definitive chemoradiation: Weekly Cisplatin 40mg/m² IV + External beam radiation (45-50Gy to pelvis + para-aortic nodes) + Brachytherapy (30-40Gy to point A). Treat HIV if positive. Smoking cessation. Post-treatment PET-CT at 3 months.
